aliases.name: Robin Goodfellow
aliases.meaning: Famous hobgoblin, also known as Puck

aliases.name: Puck
aliases.meaning: Mischievous hobgoblin from folklore and Shakespeare

description.summary: A friendly or mischievous goblin variant from English folklore, either a helpful household spirit or a harmless trickster.
description.full: A hobgoblin is a variant of goblin from English folklore, distinguished by being friendly or merely mischievous rather than malevolent. The name derives from 'hob' (a friendly sprite) combined with 'goblin.' Hobgoblins fall into two types: helpful household spirits similar to brownies who assist with chores and protect the family, or mischievous tricksters who play pranks and confuse travelers but cause no serious harm. The most famous hobgoblin is Robin Goodfellow (Puck) from Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream. Modern usage has inverted the term's meaning, often using 'hobgoblin' to mean a scary goblin, opposite to its original friendly connotation.

etymology.origin: From 'Hob' (a friendly sprite or nickname for Robert/Robin) + 'goblin'
etymology.note: Originally denoted friendly variant; modern usage often reverses this to mean scary goblin

related_phenomena.name: Brownie
related_phenomena.description: Scottish household spirit, very similar to helpful hobgoblin

appearance.description: More pleasant appearance than malevolent goblins
Can appear as small man, animal, or invisible

physical.morphology: Small humanoid, less grotesque than malevolent goblins, sometimes invisible

abilities.description: Help with cleaning, cooking, farm work at night
Can take various forms, especially animal shapes
Guard household from harm, warn of danger

types.behavior: Assists with chores, protects family, like a brownie
Plays pranks, confuses travelers, minor mischief

patterns: Work at night while household sleeps
Offended by gifts of clothing (will leave)
Protect those who treat them well

beliefs: Offended by payment or gifts of clothing
Expect respect and small offerings (food, drink)
Will leave if insulted or given clothes
Can be mischievous but not dangerous

famous_examples.name: Robin Goodfellow (Puck)
famous_examples.description: Most famous hobgoblin, mischievous fairy servant of Oberon
famous_examples.characteristics: Trickster, shape-shifter, prankster but ultimately benevolent

famous_examples.name: Hob of Runswick Bay
famous_examples.characteristics: Assisted with farm work, left when given clothing

distinction_from_goblin.modern_reversal: Modern usage often treats 'hobgoblin' as scarier or larger goblin (opposite of original meaning)
distinction_from_goblin.note: Original distinction largely lost in modern popular culture

similarities_to_brownie: Works at night on household chores
taboos: Never give clothing (causes them to leave)
Never insult or criticize their work

literature: Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream (Puck/Robin Goodfellow)
modern_interpretations: Marvel Comics (Hobgoblin villain - reverses friendly meaning)
D&D and fantasy games (often more organized/militant goblins)
Often confused with or used as synonym for goblin
popular_culture.meaning_shift: Modern popular culture has largely inverted the original meaning, now often using 'hobgoblin' for scary or dangerous goblins

range.description: English folklore, particularly rural and household traditions

origins.cultural_roots: English household spirit and goblin folklore traditions
origins.folklore_connections: Related to brownie traditions and broader fairy folklore

timeline.significance: Distinguishes friendly goblins from malevolent ones
Shakespeare popularizes Robin Goodfellow/Puck as famous hobgoblin
Further literary establishment of hobgoblin concept
Popular culture shifts 'hobgoblin' to mean scary goblin

modern_interpretation.original_meaning: Friendly or mischievous goblin, helpful household spirit
modern_interpretation.modern_usage: Often used to mean scary, dangerous, or militaristic goblin (opposite of original)
examples_of_shift: D&D hobgoblins: militaristic, organized, larger goblins (dangerous)
Popular usage: 'hobgoblin' often means something frightening
modern_interpretation.preservation_of_original: Original friendly meaning preserved mainly in folklore scholarship and traditional literature
